content:
Consider the following series:

(i) $\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$

(ii) $ \sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n(n+1)}$

(iii) $\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}$
explanation:
$$(i)\,\, \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^p} $$

By P-test: $\quad P=\frac{1}{2}<1$

$\therefore$ Series is divergent.

$$(ii)\,\, \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} u_n=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n(n+1)}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}-\frac{1}{n+1}=1-\frac{1}{n+1} $$

As $n \rightarrow \infty$

$$ =1 $$

$\therefore$ It is convergent.

(iii) $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}$

By ratio test:

$$ \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{u_n}{u_n+1}=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{1}{n!}}{\frac{1}{(n+1)!}}=\infty>1 $$

$\therefore$ It is convergent.

content:
Consider the function $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined as

$$ f(x)=2 x^3-3 x^2-12 x+1 $$

Which of the following statements is/are correct?

(Here, $\mathbb{R}$ is the set of real numbers.)
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} f(x) & =2 x^3-3 x^2-12 x+1 \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =6 x^2-6 x-12 \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =0 \\ 6 x^2-6 x-12 & =0 \\ 6\left(x^2-x-2\right) & =0 \\ 6(x-2)(x+1) & =0 \\ x & =2 \\ x & =-1 \\ f^{\prime \prime}(x) & =12 x-6 \\ \left.f^{\prime \prime}(x)\right|_{x=2} & =12 \times 2-6 \\ & =18>0 \\ x & =2 \Rightarrow \text { local minima } \\ \left.f^{\prime \prime}(x)\right|_{x=-1} & =12 \times-1-6 \\ & =-12-6 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =-18<0 \\\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, x & =-1 \Rightarrow \text { local maxima } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { So function has neither global maxima nor global minima. } $$

content:
Consider the two-dimensional vector field $$\overrightarrow F (x,y) - x\overrightarrow i + y\overrightarrow j $$, where $$\overrightarrow i $$ and $$\widehat j$$ denote the unit vectors along the x-axis and the y-axis, respectively. A contour C in the x-y plane, as shown in the figure, is composed of two horizontal lines connected at the two ends by two semicircular arcs of unit radius. The contour is traversed in the counter-clockwise sense. The value of the closed path integral

$$\oint\limits_C {\overrightarrow F (x,y)\,.\,(dx\overrightarrow i + dy\overrightarrow j )} $$

is ___________.
explanation:
$$\oint\limits_C {\vec F(x,y){\mkern 1mu} .{\mkern 1mu} (dxi + dyj)} $$

$$ = \oint\limits_C {\overrightarrow F (x\overrightarrow i + y\overrightarrow j )\,.\,(dx\overrightarrow i + dy\overrightarrow j } ) = \oint\limits_C {xdx + ydy} $$

By Green's theorem $$ = \int\!\!\!\int\limits_R {(0 - 0)dx\,dy} $$

$$ = 0$$

content:
The partial derivative of the function

$$ f(x, y, z)=e^{1-x \cos y}+x z e^{\frac{-1}{\left(1+y^2\right)}} $$

with respect to $x$ at the point $(1,0, e)$ is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given : } f(x, y, z)=e^{1-x \cos y}+x z e^{-1 /\left(1+y^2\right)} $$

Partially differentiating with respect to $x$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=e^{(1-x \cos y)}(-\cos y)+z e^{-1 /\left(1+y^2\right)} \times 1 \\ & \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=-e^{(1-x \cos y)}(-\cos y)+z e^{-1 /\left(1+y^2\right)} \end{aligned} $$

Now, finding partial derivative at point ( $1,0, e$ ),

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=-e^{(1-1)} \cos 0+e e^{-1 /(1+0)} \\ & \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=-1+1=0 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

$$P:$$ If $$f(x)$$ is continuous at $$x = {x_0},$$ then it is also differentiable at $$x = {x_0},$$
$$Q:$$ If $$f(x)$$ is continuous at $$x = {x_0},$$ then it may not be differentiable at $$x = {x_0},$$
$$R:$$ If $$f(x)$$ is differentiable at $$x = {x_0},$$ then it is also continuous at $$x = {x_0},$$

options.content: $$P$$ is true, $$Q$$ is false, $$R$$ is false

content: A function $$f\left( x \right) = 1 - {x^2} + {x^3}\,\,$$ is defined in the closed interval $$\left[ { - 1,1} \right].$$ The value of $$x,$$ in the open interval $$(-1,1)$$ for which the mean value theorem is satisfied, is
The contour on the $$x-y$$ plane, where the partial derivative of $${x^2} + {y^2}$$ with respect to $$y$$ is equal to the partial derivative of $$6y+4x$$ with respect to $$'x',$$  is
The value of $$\sum\limits_{n = 0}^\infty  {n{{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)}^n}\,\,} $$ is _______.
The value of $$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } {\left( {1 + {1 \over x}} \right)^x}\,\,$$ is
explanation:
$$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } {\left( {1 + {1 \over x}} \right)^x}$$

Let $$y = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } {\left( {1 + {1 \over x}} \right)^x}$$

Taking 'ln' on both side,

$$\ln y = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } {{\ln \left( {1 + {1 \over x}} \right)} \over {{x^{ - 1}}}}$$

$$\ln y = {{\ln (1 + 0)} \over 0} = {0 \over 0}$$

It is in $${0 \over 0}$$ form. So, applying L' Hospital's rule,

$$\ln y = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } \left[ {{{{1 \over {1 + {1 \over x}}} \times \left( {{{ - 1} \over {{x^2}}}} \right)} \over {\left( {{{ - 1} \over {{x^2}}}} \right)}}} \right]$$

$$\ln y = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } {1 \over {1 + {1 \over x}}}$$

$$\ln y = {1 \over {1 + 0}}$$

$$\ln y = 1$$

$$y = {e^1} = e$$

explanation:
The linear approximations for ex and e$$-$$x for very small values | x | << 1 are given by,

$${e^x} \simeq 1 + x$$ ...... (i)

$${e^{ - x}} \simeq 1 - x$$ .... (ii)

The ratio coth(x) is given by,

$$\coth (x) = {{\cosh (x)} \over {\sinh (x)}}$$

$$\coth (x) = {{{e^x} + {e^{ - x}}} \over {{e^x} - {e^{ - x}}}}$$

From equations (i) and (ii),

$$\coth (x) = {{1 + x + 1 - x} \over {1 + x - 1 + x}} = {2 \over {2x}} = {1 \over x}$$

Consider a non-negative function $f(x)$ which is continuous and bounded over the interval $[2,8]$. Let $M$ and $m$ denote, respectively, the maximum and the minimum values of $f(x)$ over the interval.

Among the combinations of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ given below, choose the one(s) for which the inequality

$$ \beta \leq \int_2^8 f(x) d x \leq \alpha $$

is guaranteed to hold.
explanation:
$f(x) \geq 0$ given in $[2,8]=[a, b]$

by F.T.I.C

$$ \begin{aligned} m(b-a) & \leq \int_a^b f(x) d x \leq M(b-a) \\ m(8-2) & \leq \int_2^8 f(x) d x \leq M(8-2) \\ 6 m & \leq \int_2^8 f(x) d x \leq 6 M \end{aligned} $$

$$ \because f(x) \geq 0 $$

$\Rightarrow \quad m$ and $M \geq 0$

$\therefore \quad$ The possible answer is

$$ 5 m \leq \int_2^8 f(x) d x \leq 7 M $$

content: Consider the Earth to be a perfect sphere of radius $R$. Then the surface area of the region, enclosed by the 60°N latitude circle, that contains the north pole in its interior is _______.
explanation:
To determine the surface area of the region enclosed by the $60^\circ$N latitude circle, which contains the North Pole in its interior, we need to understand the geometry of the sphere and the concept of latitude.

The latitude $60^\circ$N is $60^\circ$ from the equator. The latitude circles divide the surface of the sphere into smaller circular regions. Specifically, the latitude circle at $60^\circ$N divides the sphere into two regions: one enclosing the North Pole and one enclosing the part from $60^\circ$N to the South Pole.

The surface area of a spherical cap (the part that contains the North Pole) can be determined using the formula:

$$ A = 2 \pi R^2 (1 - \cos \phi) $$

where:

  • $R$ is the radius of the sphere
  • $\phi$ is the colatitude (the angle from the top of the sphere). For $60^\circ$N latitude, the colatitude is $30^\circ$ (i.e., $90^\circ - 60^\circ$).

Thus, we have:

$$ \phi = 30^\circ $$

We convert the angle to radians since trigonometric functions use radians:

$$ \phi = 30^\circ = \frac{\pi}{6} \, \text{radians} $$

Now we can find $\cos \phi$:

$$ \cos \left( \frac{\pi}{6} \right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

Substituting this into the area formula, we get:

$$ A = 2 \pi R^2 \left( 1 - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) $$

$$ A = 2 \pi R^2 \left( \frac{2}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) $$

$$ A = 2 \pi R^2 \cdot \frac{2 - \sqrt{3}}{2} $$

$$ A = \pi R^2 (2 - \sqrt{3}) $$

Therefore, the surface area of the region enclosed by the $60^\circ$N latitude circle that contains the North Pole in its interior is:

$$ \left( 2 - \sqrt{3} \right) \pi R^2 $$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option A: $\left( 2 - \sqrt{3} \right) \pi R^2$

content: The value of the line integral $$\int_P^Q {({z^2}dx + 3{y^2}dy + 2xz\,dz)} $$ along the straight line joining the points $$P(1,1,2)$$ and $$Q(2,3,1)$$ is
explanation:
Given integral is, $I=\int\limits_P^Q\left(z^2 d x+3 y^2 d y+2 x z d z\right)$

Also given points $P(1,1,2)$ and $Q(2,3,1)$

Equation of line joining the points P and Q is,

$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-1} \\\\ & \text { Let } \frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-1}=t \\\\ & x=t+1 \Rightarrow d x=d t \\\\ & y=2 t+1 \Rightarrow d y=2 d t \\\\ & z=2-t \Rightarrow d z=-d t \\\\ & t \text { limits } t=0 \text { to } t=1\end{aligned}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I=\int\limits_{t=0}^1\left[(2-t)^2+3(2 t+1)^2-2(t+1)(2-t)\right] d t \\\\ & I=\int\limits_{t=0}^1\left(27 t^2+18 t+6\right) d t \\\\ & I=\left[\frac{27 t^3}{3}+\frac{18 t^2}{2}+6 t\right]_0^1 \\\\ & I=\frac{27}{3}+\frac{18}{2}+6=24 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: The value of the integral $$\int\limits\!\!\!\int_R {xy\,dx\,dy} $$ over the regioin R, given in the figure, is _________ (rounded off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
Given region R can be drawn as shown below

$\begin{aligned} \therefore \iint\limits_R x y d x d y & =\iint\limits_{R_1} x y d x d y+\iint\limits_{R_2} x y d x d y \\\\ & =\int\limits_{x=-1}^0 \int\limits_{y=-x}^{x+2} x y d y d x+\int\limits_{x=0}^1 \int\limits_{y=x}^{x+2} x y d y d x \\\\ & =\int\limits_{x=-1}^0\left(\frac{x y^2}{2}\right)_{-x}^{2+x} d x+\int\limits_{x=0}^1\left(\frac{x y^2}{2}\right)_x^{2-x} d x\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\int\limits_{x=-1}^0\left(\frac{x(2+x)^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{2}\right) d x+\int\limits_{x=0}^1\left(\frac{x(2-x)^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{2}\right) d x \\\\ & =\int\limits_{-1}^0 \frac{x}{2}\left[(x+2)^2-(x)^2\right] d x+\int\limits_0^1 \frac{x}{2}\left[(2-x)^2-x^2\right] d x \\\\ & =\int\limits_{-1}^0 \frac{x}{2}\left(x^2+4 x+4-x^2\right) d x+\int\limits_0^1 \frac{x}{2}\left(x^2-4 x+4-x^2\right) d x\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\int\limits_{-1}^0 \frac{x}{2}(4 x+4) d x+\int\limits_0^1 \frac{x}{2}(4-4 x) d x \\\\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_{-1}^0\left(4 x^2+4 x\right) d x+\frac{1}{2} \int\limits_0^1\left(4 x-4 x^2\right) d x \\\\ & =\left.\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4 x^3}{3}+\frac{4 x^2}{2}\right)\right|_{-1} ^0+\left.\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4 x^2}{2}-\frac{4 x^3}{3}\right)\right|_0 ^1\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[(0+0)-\left(-\frac{4}{3}+\frac{4}{2}\right)\right]+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4}{2}-\frac{4}{3}\right) \\\\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4}{3}-\frac{4}{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4}{2}-\frac{4}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4}{3}-\frac{4}{2}+\frac{4}{2}-\frac{4}{3}\right)=0\end{aligned}$

content:
The function f(x) = 8loge x $$-$$ x2 + 3 attains its minimum over the interval [1, e] at x = __________.

(Here loge x is the natural logarithm of x.)
explanation:
Let's define the function as $ f(x) = 8\ln(x) - x^2 + 3 $. To find the stationary points, we need to calculate the first derivative of $f(x)$ and set it to zero :

$ f'(x) = \frac{8}{x} - 2x = 0 $

Solving the equation, we get :

$ 2x^2 = 8 $

$ x^2 = 4 $

$ x = \pm 2 $

Since we are only considering the interval $[1, e]$, we have $ x = 2 $. Next, we check the concavity at $ x = 2 $ using the second derivative :

$ f''(x) = -\frac{8}{x^2} - 2 $

Evaluating at $ x = 2 $ :

$ f''(2) = -\frac{8}{4} - 2 = -6 $

Since $ f''(2) < 0 $, this indicates a local maximum at $ x = 2 $. Therefore, to determine the minimum of $ f(x) $ within the interval $[1, e]$, we need to evaluate the endpoints :

$ f(1) = 8\ln(1) - 1^2 + 3 = 0 + 3 - 1 = 2 $

Next, we calculate $ f(e) $ :

$ f(e) = 8\ln(e) - e^2 + 3 $

Since $\ln(e) = 1$, this simplifies to :

$ f(e) = 8 \cdot 1 - e^2 + 3 = 8 + 3 - e^2 $

Given $ e \approx 2.718 $, thus $ e^2 \approx 7.38 $ :

$ f(e) = 8 + 3 - 7.38 = 3.62 $

Comparing $ f(1) = 2 $ and $ f(e) = 3.62 $, we find the minimum value of $ f(x) $ on the interval $[1, e]$ occurs at $ x = 1 $.

in the interval $$\,0 \le x \le 3$$ is __________.
For a right angled triangle, if the sum of the lengths of the hypotenuse and a side is kept constant, in order to have maximum area of the triangle, the angle between the hypotenuse and the side is
The Taylor series expansion of $$\,\,{{\sin x} \over {x - \pi }}\,\,$$ at $$x = \pi $$ is given by
explanation:
Taylor's series expansion of f(x) around x = $$\pi$$ is

$$f(x) = f(\pi ) + {{x - \pi } \over 1}f'(\pi ) + {{{{(x - \pi )}^2}} \over 2}f''(\pi ) + ....$$

Now, $$f(\pi ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \pi } {{\sin x} \over {x - \pi }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \pi } {{\cos x} \over 1} = - 1$$

Similarly, by using L' Hospital's rule, we can show that

$$f'(\pi ) = 0$$ and

$$f''(\pi ) = - 1/6$$

So, the expansion is $$f(x) = - 1( - 1/6){(x - \pi )^2} + ....$$

$$\therefore$$ $$f(x) = - 1 - {{{{(x - \pi )}^2}} \over {3!}} + ...$$

content: In the Taylor series expansion of $${e^x} + \sin x$$  about the point $$x = \pi ,$$ the coefficient of $${\left( {x = \pi } \right)^2}$$  is
explanation:
Given : $$f(x) = {e^x} + \sin x$$ ....... (i)

The Taylor series expansion for a function f(x) is,

$$f(x) = f(a) + (x - a)f'(a) + {{{{(x - a)}^2}} \over {2!}}f''(a) + ......\infty $$

On comparing, the coefficient of $${(x - \pi )^2}$$ is $${1 \over {2!}}f''(a)$$ [take a = $$\pi$$].

Differentiating equation (i),

$$f'(x) = {e^x} + \cos x$$

$$f''(x) = {e^x} - \sin x$$

Thus, coefficient of $${(x - \pi )^2}$$ at (x = $$\pi$$) is,

$$ = {1 \over {2!}}{\left\{ {{e^x} - \sin x} \right\}_{x = \pi }} = {1 \over 2}{e^\pi } = 0.5{e^\pi }$$

content: The value of the integral of the function $$\,\,g\left( {x,y} \right) = 4{x^3} + 10{y^4}\,\,$$  along the straight line segment from the point $$(0,0)$$ to the point $$(1,2)$$ in the $$xy$$ -plane is
Consider the function $$\,f\left( x \right) = {x^2} - x - 2.\,$$ The maximum value of $$f(x)$$ in the closed interval $$\left[ { - 4,4} \right]\,$$
